era_context: The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander (r. 222–235) faces mounting pressure on multiple frontiers. The Sassanid Empire is founded in 224 CE under Ardashir I, who has just overthrown the Parthian Arsacid dynasty; 226 marks the early consolidation of Sassanid power. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is well-established; Wei dominates the north under Cao Pi (r. 220–226), while Wu and Shu maintain independent states. This is an era of imperial transition, frontier warfare, and political consolidation across Eurasia.

events.title: Cao Pi dies; Cao Rui succeeds as Wei emperor
events.description: Cao Pi (r. 220–226), the founder of the Wei Dynasty and son of Cao Cao, dies in 226 CE. He is succeeded by his son Cao Rui (r. 226–239). Cao Pi's reign saw the formal establishment of Wei as a dynasty and the consolidation of northern China under Wei control. His death marks a succession transition, though it is smooth and well-documented. Cao Rui's succession ensures continuity of Wei's dominance in northern China. Cao Pi's reign is significant for establishing the institutional foundations of Wei that will persist through Cao Rui's reign.
key_figures: Cao Cao (Cao Pi's father, founder of Wei, died 220)
events.certainty_note: Cao Pi's death in 226 CE and Cao Rui's succession are firmly established in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Follows from 220-001 (Cao Pi becomes Wei emperor)

events.title: Sassanid consolidation under Ardashir I
events.description: Ardashir I (r. 224–241), founder of the Sassanid Empire, continues consolidating power two years after overthrowing the Parthian Arsacid dynasty in 224 CE. By 226, Sassanid control is established over Persia and Mesopotamia. Ardashir's consolidation includes administrative reorganization, military strengthening, and the establishment of Zoroastrianism as the state religion. The Sassanid Empire emerges as a major power rivaling Rome. Ardashir's reign establishes the institutional foundations of the Sassanid Empire that will persist for four centuries.
key_figures: Papak (Ardashir's father, founder of Sassanid dynasty)

sources.name: Sassanid inscriptions (Res Gestae Divi Saporis, later compilation)
Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: Ardashir I's consolidation of the Sassanid Empire by 226 is well-attested in multiple source traditions, though details vary.

events.title: Sun Quan consolidates Wu state
events.description: Sun Quan (r. 222–252), the second emperor of Wu, continues consolidating his state's administrative and military structures. Wu controls the Yangtze River valley and southern coastal regions, giving it economic advantages despite military pressure from Wei. Sun Quan's reign sees the development of Wu's naval power and administrative systems. By 226, Wu is well-established as a viable competitor to Wei despite the latter's northern dominance. Sun Quan's consolidation ensures Wu's survival as an independent state through the remainder of the Three Kingdoms period.
key_figures: Sun Ce (Sun Quan's father, founder of Wu, died 226)
events.certainty_note: Sun Quan's consolidation of Wu is well-attested in Chinese historical records for this period.
cross_references: Related to 226-001 (Cao Pi's death and Wei succession)

events.title: Roman-Sassanid frontier tensions escalate
events.description: Tensions between Rome and the newly consolidated Sassanid Empire escalate in 226. Ardashir I's consolidation of Sassanid power and his ambitions to expand westward into Mesopotamia create conflict with Rome. Severus Alexander's Roman Empire faces the challenge of a new, aggressive eastern power. The exact sequence of military operations is unclear from surviving sources, but this period marks the beginning of centuries of Sassanid-Roman conflict. Both empires begin military preparations for larger conflicts.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: Escalation of Roman-Sassanid tensions in 226 is probable given Ardashir's consolidation, but specific military operations are not clearly dated to this year in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of Sassanid-Roman military escalation to 226 vs. 227–230 is uncertain. Some sources suggest that major military operations did not begin until 230 or later. The extent of Ardashir I's territorial consolidation by 226 is also debated; some scholars argue that full Sassanid control of Mesopotamia took longer to establish. For Chinese history, the dating of Sun Quan's consolidation is well-attested, but some details of administrative reforms are uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 226 CE is moderate. Chinese sources for Cao Pi's death and Sun Quan's consolidation are well-preserved and reliable. Roman and Sassanid sources for frontier tensions are less clear; Herodian is non-contemporary (writing c. 240 CE), and the Historia Augusta is unreliable. Sassanid sources are sparse and often later inscriptions. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. Regional coverage is skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and China; Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are largely absent. The bias toward elite political narratives is pronounced.

graph_edges.note: Cao Rui's succession follows from Cao Pi's death
Ardashir's consolidation follows from Sassanid founding
Roman-Sassanid tensions escalate due to Ardashir's consolidation
Escalating tensions lead to major military conflict
Wei and Wu succession/consolidation occur simultaneously

era_context: The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander faces mounting pressure on multiple frontiers. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Ardashir I has recently consolidated power and begun aggressive expansion westward. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is well established following the collapse of the Han Dynasty; the state of Wei dominates the north under the Cao family. This is an era of imperial fragmentation, religious ferment (early Christianity spreading through the Roman world), and military competition for hegemonic control across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological evidence for this specific year)
Southeast Asia and Oceania (minimal contemporary documentation)

events.title: Sassanid invasion of Mesopotamia and Roman frontier conflict
events.description: Ardashir I of the Sassanid Empire launches military campaigns against Roman-held Mesopotamia and Syria, seeking to reclaim former Achaemenid territories. Roman Emperor Severus Alexander mobilizes forces to defend the eastern frontier. This marks the beginning of sustained Sassanid-Roman conflict that will define the next three centuries. The campaigns are partially successful for Sassanid forces, establishing Ardashir's military credibility and territorial ambitions.

sources.name: Sassanid inscriptions (Res Gestae Divi Saporis tradition)

events.certainty_note: Sassanid expansion in this period is well attested, but precise dating of individual campaigns varies by source. Most scholars place major Mesopotamian incursions in the mid-220s CE.

events.title: Wei Dynasty consolidation under Cao Pi
events.description: Cao Pi, son of Cao Cao and ruler of the state of Wei, continues consolidation of northern Chinese territories. Administrative reforms strengthen central authority and military organization. The Wei state represents the most powerful of the Three Kingdoms, controlling the economically productive Yellow River valley and maintaining a sophisticated bureaucracy. This period sees continued warfare with rival states Wu and Shu, but Wei's institutional strength gives it strategic advantage.

sources.name: Records of Wei (Wei Shu) in Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang

events.certainty_note: Wei's administrative activities in this period are well documented in Chinese historical records compiled within centuries of the events.
cross_references: Part of broader Three Kingdoms conflict (220-280 CE)

events.title: Sun Quan's consolidation of Wu territory in southern China
events.description: Sun Quan, who inherited leadership of the Wu state from his father Sun Ce, continues military campaigns to consolidate control over southern China. He faces internal challenges from regional warlords and external pressure from Wei to the north. Sun Quan's reign sees development of a distinct southern Chinese culture and economy, with emphasis on naval power and riverine control. His administrative reforms establish Wu as a viable long-term competitor in the Three Kingdoms struggle.

sources.name: Records of Wu (Wu Shu) in Sanguozhi by Chen Shou

events.certainty_note: Wu's consolidation activities are well documented in Chinese historical sources.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)

events.title: Roman military crisis and frontier instability
events.description: Severus Alexander's reign is marked by persistent military challenges across the empire's vast frontiers. Germanic tribes press the Rhine frontier; Sassanid forces threaten the east; internal military discipline deteriorates. The emperor attempts to address these through diplomatic and military means, but resource constraints and the army's growing political power create systemic instability. This year represents the broader crisis of the 220s-230s CE, when the Roman Empire's military capacity is severely tested.
key_figures: Ulpian (jurist and administrator, died 223 CE but influence continues)

sources.name: Dio Cassius, Roman History (as preserved in later compilations)
Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: The general crisis of Roman frontiers in this period is well attested, though specific events in 225 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years in sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman military crisis (220-235 CE)
Led to 235-001 (assassination of Severus Alexander and Crisis of the Third Century)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating and significance of Sassanid campaigns in 225 CE versus 224 CE or 226 CE is debated among scholars. Some sources place Ardashir's major Mesopotamian invasion slightly later (226-227 CE). The Historia Augusta, a primary source for Severus Alexander's reign, is known to contain legendary and unreliable material, making precise chronology difficult. Modern scholarship increasingly questions whether Severus Alexander's eastern campaign was as militarily successful as traditional accounts suggest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 225 CE is heavily skewed toward Roman and Chinese sources. Roman history relies heavily on the Historia Augusta, a 4th-century compilation of dubious reliability, supplemented by later historians like Herodian and Dio Cassius. Chinese history benefits from the comprehensive Sanguozhi and later compilations like the Zizhi Tongjian, which provide detailed administrative and military records. Sassanid history is known primarily through later Persian sources and Roman accounts of Sassanid actions, creating a perspective bias. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are essentially absent from surviving documentation for this specific year. The record privileges elite political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Sassanid expansion initiated in 224 CE
Prompted Severus Alexander's eastern military campaign
Competing consolidation efforts in Three Kingdoms period
Military instability contributed to Severus Alexander's assassination and subsequent Crisis of the Third Century

era_context: Ardashir I has recently defeated the last Parthian king Artabanus V and established the Sassanid Persian Empire, fundamentally reshaping the political landscape of western Asia. The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander faces the consequences of this new, more centralized and aggressive Persian state. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is firmly established; Wei dominates the north, while Wu and Shu consolidate their respective territories. This is a pivotal year for the emergence of the Sassanid Empire as a major imperial power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indian subcontinent (limited specific documentation for this year)

events.title: Ardashir I defeats Artabanus V and establishes Sassanid Empire
events.description: Ardashir I, founder of the Sassanid dynasty, defeats and kills Artabanus V, the last Parthian Arsacid king, in the Battle of Hormozdgan (or Ctesiphon region). This victory marks the end of the Parthian Empire (247 BCE – 224 CE) and the establishment of the Sassanid Persian Empire, which will dominate western Asia for four centuries. Ardashir consolidates control over the former Parthian territories and establishes a centralized, bureaucratic state with Zoroastrianism as the state religion. This represents one of the most significant political transformations in ancient history, replacing a decentralized feudal system with a centralized imperial administration.

sources.name: Sassanid inscriptions, particularly the Res Gestae Divi Saporis (Deeds of Shapur I, Ardashir's son, which references his father's victories)
Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-l-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: The defeat of Artabanus V and establishment of the Sassanid Empire is one of the most securely dated events of this period, attested in multiple independent sources and numismatic evidence. The exact date of 224 CE is widely accepted by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Led to 225-001 (Sassanid expansion into Roman territories)
Part of broader transformation of western Asian geopolitics (220-240 CE)

events.title: Cao Cao's death and succession of Cao Pi
events.description: Cao Cao, the great warlord who unified northern China and founded the state of Wei, dies. His son Cao Pi succeeds him and continues the consolidation of Wei power. Cao Pi's succession is contested by some family members, but he emerges as the dominant figure. This transition marks a shift from the military genius of Cao Cao to the more administrative approach of Cao Pi, who will eventually proclaim himself emperor and formally establish the Wei Dynasty in 220 CE (though some sources date this to 226 CE). The succession is significant for the future trajectory of the Three Kingdoms period.
key_figures: Cao Cao (founder of Wei, died 220 CE)
Cao Pi (successor, son of Cao Cao)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou

events.certainty_note: Cao Cao's death is well documented in Chinese historical records. However, the exact year is sometimes given as 220 CE in some sources, creating minor chronological variation. Most modern scholarship accepts 220 CE as the death date, making this entry potentially misdated. This is flagged in disconfirming evidence.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)
Led to 226-001 (Cao Pi's proclamation as emperor)

events.title: Roman-Sassanid diplomatic tensions escalate
events.description: Following Ardashir I's consolidation of power, diplomatic tensions between the Roman Empire and the newly established Sassanid Empire escalate. The Sassanid state, more centralized and aggressive than the previous Parthian Empire, begins asserting claims to former Achaemenid territories in Mesopotamia and Syria. Roman Emperor Severus Alexander attempts diplomatic engagement but prepares for military conflict. This marks the beginning of a centuries-long rivalry between Rome and Sassanid Persia.
events.certainty_note: The escalation of tensions is well attested, though specific diplomatic events in 224 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.

events.title: Ulpian's influence on Roman law and administration peaks
events.description: Ulpian, the influential Roman jurist and administrator, reaches the height of his power and influence under Severus Alexander. He serves as praetorian prefect and chief legal advisor to the emperor. Ulpian's legal writings and administrative reforms shape Roman law and governance during this period. His emphasis on rational jurisprudence and imperial authority influences the development of Roman legal thought. However, his power and influence create resentment among the military, setting the stage for his assassination in 223 CE (or possibly 224 CE, sources vary).

sources.name: Digest of Justinian (Digesta), which preserves Ulpian's legal writings

events.certainty_note: Ulpian's influence during Severus Alexander's reign is well attested, but his death date is disputed between 223 and 224 CE. The exact chronology of his final years is uncertain.
cross_references: Led to 223-001 (Ulpian's assassination, if dated to 223 CE) or occurred in same year (if dated to 224 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Cao Cao's death is problematic in this entry. Most authoritative sources place Cao Cao's death in 220 CE, not 224 CE. If this entry is dated to 224 CE, it is anachronistic. The succession of Cao Pi occurred in 220 CE, making this event misdated by four years. Ulpian's death date is disputed: some sources place it in 223 CE, others in 224 CE, and some even later. The Historia Augusta's account of Severus Alexander's reign contains legendary material and is considered unreliable by modern scholars. The exact dating of Ardashir I's victory over Artabanus V is sometimes given as 226 CE in some sources, though 224 CE is the scholarly consensus.
historiographic_note: 224 CE is a pivotal year in western Asian history but is documented primarily through later sources. The Sassanid victory is attested in Sassanid inscriptions (contemporary) and later Persian and Islamic histories (non-contemporary). Roman history relies on the Historia Augusta and later historians, which are of variable reliability. Chinese history is well documented through the Sanguozhi and later compilations. The record privileges political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments. The perspective is heavily weighted toward elite actors and state-level politics. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Ardashir's consolidation prompted Roman-Sassanid tensions
Ardashir's consolidation led to military expansion into Roman territories
Cao Pi's succession led to his proclamation as emperor
Ulpian's death date is disputed between 223 and 224 CE

era_context: The Sassanid Empire under Ardashir I is consolidating its control over former Parthian territories and establishing itself as a major imperial power. The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander faces mounting military and political pressures. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is well established, with Wei, Wu, and Shu competing for dominance. This is a year of transition and consolidation across multiple empires.

events.title: Ulpian's assassination or death
events.description: Ulpian, the influential Roman jurist and praetorian prefect, is assassinated or dies under unclear circumstances. Sources vary on whether his death occurred in 223 CE or 224 CE. Ulpian had become increasingly powerful under Severus Alexander, which created resentment among the military and other factions. His death marks a significant loss of intellectual influence on Roman law and administration. The circumstances of his death reflect the growing instability and factional conflict within the Roman imperial government.

sources.name: Digest of Justinian, which preserves Ulpian's legal writings

events.certainty_note: Ulpian's death is attested in multiple sources, but the exact year is disputed between 223 and 224 CE. The circumstances are unclear, and sources provide conflicting accounts.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman political instability (220-235 CE)

events.title: Sassanid administrative consolidation under Ardashir I
events.description: Ardashir I continues consolidating the Sassanid Empire's administrative structure. He establishes a centralized bureaucracy, reorganizes the military, and promotes Zoroastrianism as the state religion. The Sassanid state develops a sophisticated system of provincial administration and tax collection, replacing the more decentralized Parthian system. This administrative consolidation strengthens the empire's capacity for sustained military campaigns and long-term imperial governance.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Sassanid administrative consolidation is well attested in general terms, but specific events in 223 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Sassanid state-building (224-240 CE)

events.title: Liu Bei's establishment of Shu Han in southern China
events.description: Liu Bei, a warlord claiming descent from the Han imperial house, consolidates control over southwestern China and establishes the state of Shu Han. Liu Bei's state represents a rival power to Wei in the north and Wu in the south. Shu Han develops a distinct political identity based on Liu Bei's claim to legitimacy as a Han successor and his reputation for benevolent governance. The establishment of Shu Han solidifies the Three Kingdoms period as a stable tripartite division of China.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's establishment of Shu Han is well documented in Chinese historical records. The date of 221 CE is sometimes given for the formal proclamation of Shu Han, but 223 CE represents the consolidation of his control.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)

events.title: Roman military instability and frontier pressures
events.description: The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander continues to face military pressures on multiple frontiers. Germanic tribes press the Rhine frontier; Sassanid forces threaten the east; internal military discipline deteriorates. The assassination or death of Ulpian removes a stabilizing administrative influence. The military becomes increasingly politicized and factional, with different units supporting different factions within the imperial government. This instability foreshadows the Crisis of the Third Century.
key_figures: Ulpian (Roman administrator, died this year or 224 CE)

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: The general military instability of this period is well attested, though specific events in 223 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman military crisis (220-235 CE)
Led to 235-001 (assassination of Severus Alexander and Crisis of the Third Century)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Liu Bei's establishment of Shu Han is disputed. Some sources place the formal proclamation of Shu Han in 221 CE, others in 223 CE. The exact chronology of Liu Bei's consolidation of southwestern China is not always clear in surviving sources. Ulpian's death date is disputed between 223 and 224 CE, with some sources placing it even later. The Historia Augusta's account of Severus Alexander's reign contains legendary material and is considered unreliable by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: 223 CE is a year of significant political transitions across multiple empires, but documentation is heavily dependent on later sources. Roman history relies on the Historia Augusta and later historians, which are of variable reliability. Chinese history is well documented through the Sanguozhi and later compilations. Sassanid history is known primarily through later Persian sources and Roman accounts. The record privileges elite political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are absent from surviving documentation.

Military instability contributed to Severus Alexander's assassination

era_context: The Sassanid Empire under Ardashir I is consolidating its control and establishing itself as a major imperial power in western Asia. The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander faces mounting military and political pressures. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is well established, with Wei, Wu, and Shu competing for dominance. This is a year of continued consolidation and military competition across multiple empires.

events.title: Sassanid military campaigns in Mesopotamia and Syria
events.description: Ardashir I continues military campaigns against Roman-held territories in Mesopotamia and Syria. These campaigns are part of Ardashir's broader strategy to reclaim former Achaemenid territories and establish Sassanid hegemony in western Asia. The campaigns are partially successful, with Sassanid forces capturing some Roman-held cities and territories. Roman Emperor Severus Alexander prepares a military response, but the campaign is delayed by internal political instability.
events.certainty_note: Sassanid military campaigns in this period are well attested, but precise dating of individual campaigns varies by source.
cross_references: Part of broader Sassanid expansion (224-240 CE)

events.title: Wei-Wu military conflict in Three Kingdoms period
events.description: Military conflict between the states of Wei and Wu continues as part of the ongoing Three Kingdoms struggle. Cao Pi's Wei forces engage with Sun Quan's Wu forces in battles for control of central China. The conflict is characterized by naval warfare on the Yangtze River and land campaigns in the border regions. Neither side achieves decisive victory, but the conflict establishes the military balance that will characterize the Three Kingdoms period.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou

events.certainty_note: Wei-Wu military conflict during this period is well documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)

events.title: Shu Han consolidation under Liu Bei and Zhuge Liang
events.description: Liu Bei and his strategist Zhuge Liang continue consolidating control over southwestern China. Shu Han develops administrative structures and military organization to compete with Wei and Wu. The state's economy is based on agricultural production in the Sichuan basin and control of trade routes. Zhuge Liang's administrative reforms strengthen Shu Han's capacity for sustained competition in the Three Kingdoms struggle.
events.certainty_note: Shu Han's consolidation is well documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)

events.title: Roman political instability and succession disputes
events.description: Severus Alexander's reign continues to be marked by political instability and succession disputes. The emperor's mother, Julia Maesa, wields significant influence over imperial policy. The military becomes increasingly factional and politicized, with different units supporting different factions. The imperial government struggles to maintain coherent policy in the face of military pressures on multiple frontiers and internal political conflict.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: The general political instability of Severus Alexander's reign is well attested, though specific events in 222 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman political crisis (220-235 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of individual Sassanid military campaigns in the early 220s CE is disputed among scholars. Some sources place major campaigns in 224-225 CE rather than 222 CE. The Historia Augusta's account of Severus Alexander's reign contains legendary material and is considered unreliable by modern scholars. The exact chronology of Wei-Wu military conflicts is not always clear in surviving sources, and specific battles in 222 CE may be conflated with events in surrounding years.
historiographic_note: 222 CE is a year of continued military and political competition across multiple empires, but documentation is heavily dependent on later sources. Roman history relies on the Historia Augusta and later historians, which are of variable reliability. Chinese history is well documented through the Sanguozhi and later compilations. Sassanid history is known primarily through later Persian sources and Roman accounts. The record privileges elite political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are absent from surviving documentation.

Wei-Wu conflict and Shu Han consolidation were concurrent in Three Kingdoms period
Political instability contributed to Severus Alexander's assassination

era_context: The Sassanid Empire under Ardashir I is consolidating its control over former Parthian territories. The Roman Empire under Severus Alexander faces mounting military and political pressures. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is becoming firmly established following the formal collapse of the Han Dynasty. This is a pivotal year for the emergence of the Three Kingdoms as distinct political entities and the consolidation of the Sassanid Empire.

events.title: Liu Bei proclaims himself emperor and establishes Shu Han
events.description: Liu Bei, a warlord claiming descent from the Han imperial house, formally proclaims himself emperor and establishes the state of Shu Han. This proclamation marks the formal establishment of one of the Three Kingdoms and represents a direct challenge to Cao Pi's Wei and Sun Quan's Wu. Liu Bei's claim to legitimacy is based on his descent from the Han imperial family and his reputation for benevolent governance. The establishment of Shu Han solidifies the Three Kingdoms period as a stable tripartite division of China, with each state claiming legitimacy as a successor to the Han Dynasty.
key_figures: Liu Bei (founder and emperor of Shu Han)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi) by Luo Guanzhong

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's proclamation as emperor of Shu Han in 221 CE is well documented in Chinese historical records. This is one of the most securely dated events of the Three Kingdoms period.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)
Contemporary with 221-002 (Cao Pi's proclamation as emperor of Wei)

events.title: Cao Pi proclaims himself emperor and establishes Wei Dynasty
events.description: Cao Pi, son of Cao Cao and ruler of the state of Wei, formally proclaims himself emperor and establishes the Wei Dynasty. This proclamation marks the formal end of the Han Dynasty and the establishment of Wei as a legitimate imperial state. Cao Pi's proclamation is based on his military and administrative achievements and his control of the economically productive Yellow River valley. The establishment of Wei as an imperial state solidifies the Three Kingdoms period as a tripartite division of China, with each state claiming legitimacy as a successor to the Han Dynasty.
key_figures: Cao Pi (founder and emperor of Wei)
events.certainty_note: Cao Pi's proclamation as emperor of Wei in 221 CE is well documented in Chinese historical records. However, some sources date this event to 220 CE, creating minor chronological variation.
cross_references: Part of Three Kingdoms period (220-280 CE)
Contemporary with 221-001 (Liu Bei's proclamation as emperor of Shu Han)

events.title: Sassanid consolidation and administrative reforms
events.description: Ardashir I continues consolidating the Sassanid Empire's administrative structure and military organization. The Sassanid state develops a sophisticated system of provincial administration, tax collection, and military organization. Zoroastrianism is promoted as the state religion, and the Sassanid nobility is reorganized into a hierarchical system. These administrative reforms strengthen the empire's capacity for sustained military campaigns and long-term imperial governance.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Sassanid administrative consolidation is well attested in general terms, but specific events in 221 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Sassanid state-building (224-240 CE)

events.title: Roman military preparations for eastern campaign
events.description: Severus Alexander begins military preparations for a campaign against Sassanid Persia. The emperor mobilizes forces and prepares to defend Roman territories in Mesopotamia and Syria against Sassanid expansion. However, the campaign is delayed by internal political instability and military discipline problems. The preparations reflect the growing threat posed by the newly consolidated Sassanid Empire under Ardashir I.

sources.name: Herodian, History of the Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: Roman military preparations for an eastern campaign are attested in sources, but the exact timing and scope of preparations in 221 CE are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman-Sassanid conflict (220-240 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Cao Pi's proclamation as emperor is disputed. Some sources place this event in 220 CE rather than 221 CE. The exact chronology of Liu Bei's proclamation is also subject to minor variation in sources. The Historia Augusta's account of Severus Alexander's reign contains legendary material and is considered unreliable by modern scholars. The precise dating of Sassanid administrative reforms is not always clear in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: 221 CE is a pivotal year for the establishment of the Three Kingdoms as distinct political entities and the consolidation of the Sassanid Empire. Chinese history is well documented through the Sanguozhi and later compilations, which provide detailed records of the Three Kingdoms period. Roman history relies on the Historia Augusta and later historians, which are of variable reliability. Sassanid history is known primarily through later Persian sources and Roman accounts. The record privileges elite political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments. Sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia are absent from surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Liu Bei's and Cao Pi's proclamations as emperors occurred in the same year, solidifying the Three Kingdoms period
Cao Pi's establishment of Wei led to continued consolidation and military campaigns
Sassanid consolidation led to military expansion into Roman territories
Military preparations led to Severus Alexander's eastern campaign

era_context: The Han Dynasty is in terminal decline; the Yellow Turban Rebellion (184–205 CE) has shattered central authority. Regional warlords control vast territories nominally under the Han court. The period of the Three Kingdoms (220–280 CE) is beginning. In the Mediterranean, the Roman Empire under Elagabalus faces internal instability. In South Asia, the Satavahana Empire is fragmenting. In China, the formal partition into three competing states is imminent.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record for 220 CE specifically)
Mesoamerica (Maya records exist but are sparse for this exact year)

events.title: Cao Cao proclaimed King of Wei; formal end of Han Dynasty authority in north
events.description: Cao Cao, the dominant warlord of northern China, is formally proclaimed King of Wei by the Han court—a title that effectively acknowledges his independent sovereignty while maintaining nominal Han suzerainty. This marks the practical end of centralized Han authority in the north and accelerates the fragmentation into three competing kingdoms. Cao Cao's son Cao Pi will formally depose the Han emperor in 220 CE (or 221 CE depending on source), establishing the Wei Dynasty. This event crystallizes decades of warlord competition following the Yellow Turban Rebellion.
key_figures: Cao Cao (King of Wei, military commander)

sources.name: Records of Wei (Wei Lüe) and Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang (11th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The date 220 CE is conventional for Cao Cao's kingship; some sources place the formal proclamation in 216 CE. The deposition of the Han emperor by Cao Pi occurred in 220 or 221 CE depending on the source tradition. Dating is secure to within 1–2 years.
cross_references: Leads to 221-001 (Cao Pi founds Wei Dynasty)

events.title: Liu Bei establishes base in Yizhou (Sichuan); consolidates southwestern territory
events.description: Liu Bei, a warlord claiming descent from the Han imperial house, consolidates control over Yizhou (modern Sichuan province) after years of military campaigns. With the strategist Zhuge Liang as his advisor, Liu Bei establishes a stable territorial base that will become the foundation of the Later Han (Shu) state. This consolidation is crucial to the three-way balance of power that defines the subsequent Three Kingdoms period. Yizhou's agricultural wealth and defensible terrain make it a viable independent power base.
key_figures: Liu Bei (warlord, later emperor of Shu)
Zhuge Liang (strategist, Prime Minister of Shu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi), attributed to Luo Guanzhong (14th century CE, but based on earlier sources)

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou occurred over several years (c. 214–220 CE). The year 220 CE is conventional for the completion of this process. Some sources date the final conquest of Liu Zhang to 214 CE.
cross_references: Leads to 221-002 (Liu Bei proclaims himself emperor of Shu)

events.title: Sun Ce's successor Sun Quan consolidates control of southern territories (Jiangdong)
events.description: Sun Quan, who inherited control of the Jiangdong region from his father Sun Ce and brother Sun Strategy, consolidates his authority over the wealthy southern territories. By 220 CE, Sun Quan has secured his position against internal rivals and external threats, establishing the territorial base that will become the Eastern Wu state. The southern region's economic resources and geographic isolation from northern warlords give Sun Quan strategic advantages. This consolidation completes the three-way territorial division that characterizes the Three Kingdoms period.
key_figures: Sun Quan (warlord, later emperor of Eastern Wu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Sun Quan's consolidation was gradual, occurring over the period 200–220 CE. By 220 CE, his authority was secure. The exact year is conventional rather than precisely documented.
cross_references: Leads to 222-001 (Sun Quan proclaimed King of Wu)

events.title: Elagabalus becomes Roman Emperor; religious and political upheaval
events.region: Roman Empire (centered on Rome and Syria)
events.description: Elagabalus (Varius Avitus Bassianus), a Syrian priest of the sun god Elagabal, becomes Roman Emperor at approximately age 14, following the assassination of Macrinus. His reign is marked by religious innovation (attempting to elevate the cult of Elagabal above traditional Roman religion), sexual scandal (by Roman standards), and political instability. The Severan dynasty is fragmenting. Elagabalus's reign lasts only four years (220–222 CE) and is characterized by rapid turnover of officials, military discontent, and factional conflict. His reign illustrates the instability of the third-century Roman Empire.

sources.name: Historia Augusta (Augustan History), compiled in 4th century CE
Cassius Dio, Roman History (written early 3rd century CE, covers this period)

events.certainty_note: Elagabalus's accession date is well-attested in Roman sources, though the Historia Augusta is notoriously unreliable for details. The year 220 CE is secure.
cross_references: Leads to 222-002 (Elagabalus assassinated; Severus Alexander becomes emperor)

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Ardashir I consolidates power; Parthian Empire in decline
events.description: Ardashir I, founder of the Sassanid Persian Empire, continues consolidating power in Persia and Mesopotamia, effectively ending Parthian Arsacid rule. By 220 CE, Ardashir has defeated the last Parthian king, Artabanus V, and established the Sassanid dynasty as the dominant power in the Near East. This marks a major geopolitical shift: the Sassanids will be Rome's primary rival for the next four centuries. Ardashir's reign emphasizes Zoroastrianism, centralized administration, and military reorganization.
key_figures: Artabanus V (last Parthian king, defeated c. 224 CE)

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century CE, based on earlier traditions)
Cassius Dio and later Roman historians (accounts of Sassanid rise)

events.certainty_note: Ardashir's rise occurred over the period 208–224 CE. The exact year of his consolidation is uncertain; 220 CE is approximate. The final defeat of Artabanus V is dated to c. 224 CE by most sources.
cross_references: Leads to 224-001 (Ardashir defeats Artabanus V; Sassanid Empire formally established)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional date of Cao Cao's kingship (220 CE) is disputed by some scholars, who place it in 216 CE. The exact year of Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou is debated; some sources favor 214 CE. Elagabalus's religious policies are often exaggerated in the Historia Augusta; modern scholarship suggests his reign was less scandalous than portrayed, though still unstable. The Sassanid conquest of Parthia is traditionally dated to 224 CE, not 220 CE; the year 220 CE represents the consolidation phase rather than the final military victory.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 220 CE is heavily weighted toward China and the Roman Empire, where written sources are abundant. Chinese sources (Sanguozhi, later compilations) are detailed but compiled centuries after the events. Roman sources are similarly late and often unreliable (Historia Augusta). South Asian, African, and Mesoamerican sources for this year are sparse or absent from standard historiography. The Three Kingdoms period is well-documented in Chinese sources, but the exact dating of events within the 220–222 CE transition is sometimes uncertain. Western bias in surviving sources is significant.

graph_edges.note: Cao Cao's kingship directly led to his son Cao Pi's deposition of the Han emperor and founding of Wei Dynasty
Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou enabled his proclamation as emperor of Shu in 221 CE
Sun Quan's consolidation led to his formal proclamation as King of Wu
Elagabalus's unstable reign led to his assassination and replacement by Severus Alexander
Cao Cao's northern consolidation and Liu Bei's southwestern consolidation occurred simultaneously, defining the three-way balance
Roman instability under Elagabalus coincided with Sassanid consolidation, shifting Near Eastern balance of power

era_context: The Han Dynasty is in its final year of nominal authority; the Three Kingdoms period is crystallizing. Cao Cao dominates northern China; Liu Bei and Sun Quan control southwestern and southern territories respectively. In the Roman Empire, Elagabalus is in his second year as emperor, facing military and political opposition. The Sassanid Empire is consolidating power in Persia. This is a year of transition and consolidation across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe (sparse archaeological record for this specific year)
Mesoamerica (Maya records exist but are sparse for 219 CE)

events.title: Guan Yu's military campaign and death; loss of Jingzhou to Sun Quan
events.region: Central China (Jingzhou region, Yangtze River valley)
events.description: Guan Yu, one of Liu Bei's most celebrated generals, launches a military campaign to expand Liu Bei's territory northward from Jingzhou. However, Sun Quan's forces, under the command of Lü Meng, attack from the south and capture Jingzhou while Guan Yu is engaged in the north. Guan Yu is defeated and executed by Sun Quan's forces. This loss is catastrophic for Liu Bei's strategic position and marks a major shift in the balance of power between Shu and Wu. Guan Yu's death becomes a legendary moment in Chinese history and literature, immortalized in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi), attributed to Luo Guanzhong (14th century CE)

events.certainty_note: Guan Yu's death in 219 CE is well-attested in Chinese sources. The exact circumstances are detailed in Sanguozhi and later compilations. The date is secure.
cross_references: Follows from 220-002 (Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou)
Leads to 221-002 (Liu Bei's response and proclamation as emperor)

events.title: Cao Cao's campaign against Ma Chao and Han Sui in the northwest
events.description: Cao Cao launches a major military campaign against the warlords Ma Chao and Han Sui in the Guanzhong region (modern Shaanxi). After initial setbacks, Cao Cao defeats the coalition and consolidates control over northwestern China. This campaign extends Cao Cao's territorial control and eliminates a significant rival. The campaign demonstrates Cao Cao's military superiority and his ability to project power across vast distances. The victory solidifies Cao Cao's position as the dominant northern warlord.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Cao Cao's campaign against Ma Chao and Han Sui is dated to 219 CE in most sources, though some sources place it in 217–218 CE. The dating is secure to within 1–2 years.
cross_references: Contemporary with 219-001 (Guan Yu's campaign and death)
Leads to 220-001 (Cao Cao's consolidation as King of Wei)

events.title: Elagabalus attempts religious reforms; conflict with Roman traditionalists
events.description: Elagabalus continues his attempts to elevate the cult of the Syrian sun god Elagabal above traditional Roman religion, including Jupiter. He attempts to move the sacred stone of the god to a new temple and promote Elagabal worship among the Roman elite. These religious innovations provoke strong opposition from traditionalist senators and the military, who view them as sacrilege and a threat to Roman identity. The conflict between Elagabalus and the Roman establishment intensifies, contributing to the instability of his reign.

sources.name: Historia Augusta (Augustan History), compiled in 4th century CE

events.certainty_note: Elagabalus's religious reforms are attested in later sources, but the Historia Augusta is notoriously unreliable. The exact year of specific reforms is uncertain; 219 CE is approximate.
cross_references: Leads to 222-002 (Elagabalus assassinated; religious reforms reversed)

events.title: Sassanid consolidation continues; Ardashir I defeats remaining Parthian forces
events.description: Ardashir I continues his military campaigns against the declining Parthian Arsacid Empire, defeating remaining Parthian forces and consolidating Sassanid control over Mesopotamia and Persia. By 219 CE, Ardashir has effectively eliminated Parthian power in most regions, though the final defeat of Artabanus V is traditionally dated to 224 CE. The Sassanid Empire emerges as the dominant power in the Near East, replacing the Parthian Empire that had ruled for nearly five centuries.

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Ardashir's campaigns is uncertain. The year 219 CE is approximate; the final defeat of Artabanus V is traditionally dated to 224 CE.
cross_references: Leads to 224-001 (Final defeat of Artabanus V)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Guan Yu's death is traditionally given as 219 CE, but some modern scholars argue for 220 CE based on alternative source interpretations. The exact circumstances of his capture and execution are embellished in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms; the Sanguozhi provides a more restrained account. Elagabalus's religious reforms are often exaggerated in the Historia Augusta; modern scholarship suggests they were less radical than portrayed. The Sassanid conquest of Parthia is a gradual process; the year 219 CE represents one phase rather than a decisive moment.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 219 CE is dominated by Chinese sources (Sanguozhi and later compilations), which are detailed but compiled centuries after the events. Roman sources are similarly late and often unreliable. The Three Kingdoms period is well-documented in Chinese historiography, but the exact dating of military campaigns is sometimes uncertain. South Asian, African, and Mesoamerican sources for this year are sparse or absent. The loss of Jingzhou is a pivotal moment in Chinese history, extensively documented in later literature but less so in contemporary sources.

Cao Cao's military victories consolidated his position as dominant northern warlord, leading to his kingship

era_context: The Han Dynasty is in its final years; the Three Kingdoms period is consolidating. Cao Cao dominates northern China; Liu Bei and Sun Quan control southwestern and southern territories. In the Roman Empire, Elagabalus is in his first year as emperor, facing significant opposition. The Sassanid Empire is consolidating power in Persia. This is a year of political transition and military consolidation across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (Maya records sparse for this year)

events.title: Elagabalus becomes Roman Emperor; assassination of Macrinus
events.region: Roman Empire (centered on Rome and Syria)
events.description: Elagabalus (Varius Avitus Bassianus), a young Syrian priest of the sun god Elagabal, becomes Roman Emperor following the assassination of Macrinus. The transition is orchestrated by Julia Maesa, Elagabalus's grandmother, who leverages the support of the Syrian legions and claims that Elagabalus is the illegitimate son of Caracalla. Macrinus, who had ruled for only 14 months, is defeated and executed. Elagabalus's accession marks a dramatic shift in Roman religious and political culture, as he attempts to elevate the cult of Elagabal above traditional Roman religion. His reign is characterized by instability, religious innovation, and conflict with the Roman establishment.

sources.name: Historia Augusta (Augustan History), compiled in 4th century CE
Cassius Dio, Roman History (written early 3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Elagabalus's accession in 218 CE is well-attested in Roman sources. The Historia Augusta is notoriously unreliable for details, but the basic facts of his accession are confirmed by Cassius Dio and other sources. The year 218 CE is secure.
cross_references: Follows from 217-001 (Caracalla's assassination; Macrinus becomes emperor)
Leads to 220-004 (Elagabalus's reign continues; religious conflicts intensify)

events.title: Cao Cao consolidates control over northern China; defeats remaining rivals
events.description: Cao Cao continues his military campaigns to consolidate control over northern China, defeating remaining warlords and rivals. By 218 CE, Cao Cao has effectively eliminated most significant opposition in the north and established a stable, centralized administration. His military victories and administrative reforms create a powerful state that dominates northern China. Cao Cao's consolidation is facilitated by his military genius, administrative ability, and the support of talented strategists and generals.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Cao Cao's consolidation of northern China was a gradual process occurring over many years (c. 190–220 CE). The year 218 CE represents one phase of this consolidation. The exact dating of specific campaigns is sometimes uncertain.
cross_references: Follows from 220-001 (Cao Cao proclaimed King of Wei)
Leads to 220-001 (Cao Cao proclaimed King of Wei)

events.title: Liu Bei continues consolidation of Yizhou; Zhuge Liang's strategic influence grows
events.description: Liu Bei continues his consolidation of Yizhou (modern Sichuan province) with the strategic guidance of Zhuge Liang. By 218 CE, Liu Bei has secured control over most of Yizhou and is establishing a stable administrative structure. Zhuge Liang's influence over Liu Bei's policies grows, and he becomes the primary architect of Shu's strategic vision. The consolidation of Yizhou provides Liu Bei with a secure territorial base and the economic resources necessary to compete with Cao Cao and Sun Quan.
key_figures: Liu Bei (warlord, later emperor of Shu)
Zhuge Liang (strategist, Prime Minister of Shu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou occurred over several years (c. 214–220 CE). The year 218 CE represents one phase of this consolidation. The exact dating is uncertain.
cross_references: Follows from 220-002 (Liu Bei establishes base in Yizhou)

events.title: Sun Quan consolidates control of southern territories; administrative reforms
events.description: Sun Quan continues consolidating his authority over the southern territories (Jiangdong and Jiangnan regions). By 218 CE, Sun Quan has secured his position against internal rivals and is implementing administrative reforms to strengthen his state. The southern region's economic resources and geographic isolation from northern warlords provide Sun Quan with strategic advantages. Sun Quan's consolidation is facilitated by talented administrators and generals, including Lu Su and others.
key_figures: Sun Quan (warlord, later emperor of Eastern Wu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Sun Quan's consolidation was gradual, occurring over the period 200–220 CE. The year 218 CE represents one phase of this consolidation. The exact dating is uncertain.

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Ardashir I consolidates power; military campaigns continue
events.description: Ardashir I continues his military campaigns to consolidate Sassanid power in Persia and Mesopotamia. By 218 CE, Ardashir has defeated most Parthian forces and is establishing the Sassanid Empire as the dominant power in the Near East. The Sassanid Empire's rise marks a major geopolitical shift, as it replaces the Parthian Empire as Rome's primary rival in the Near East. Ardashir's reign emphasizes Zoroastrianism, centralized administration, and military reorganization.

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century CE)

The Historia Augusta's account of Elagabalus is heavily embellished and unreliable; modern scholarship suggests his reign was less scandalous than portrayed. Cao Cao's consolidation of northern China was a gradual process; the year 218 CE represents one phase rather than a decisive moment. The Sassanid conquest of Parthia is a gradual process; the year 218 CE represents one phase rather than a final victory.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 218 CE is dominated by Chinese sources (Sanguozhi and later compilations) and Roman sources (Historia Augusta, Cassius Dio). Both are detailed but compiled centuries after the events. Chinese sources are generally more reliable than the Historia Augusta. South Asian, African, and Mesoamerican sources for this year are sparse or absent. era_context: The Han Dynasty is in terminal decline; warlords compete for supremacy in China. Cao Cao dominates the north; Liu Bei and Sun Quan are consolidating power in the southwest and south. In the Roman Empire, Caracalla is assassinated and Macrinus becomes emperor, initiating a period of instability. The Parthian Empire is declining; Ardashir I is rising in Persia. This is a year of political upheaval and military consolidation across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (Maya records sparse for this year)

events.title: Caracalla assassinated; Macrinus becomes Roman Emperor
events.region: Roman Empire (centered on Rome and Mesopotamia)
events.description: Caracalla, the Roman Emperor and son of Septimius Severus, is assassinated by members of the Praetorian Guard, including the prefect Macrinus. Caracalla's reign (211–217 CE) was marked by military campaigns, the Constitutio Antoniniana (which granted citizenship to all free inhabitants of the empire), and financial strain from military spending. Macrinus, a non-aristocratic prefect, becomes emperor—the first emperor not from the senatorial class. Macrinus's reign is brief (217–218 CE) and marked by military discontent, financial crisis, and political instability. His assassination of Caracalla and assumption of power mark a major disruption in the Severan dynasty.

sources.name: Historia Augusta (Augustan History), compiled in 4th century CE
Cassius Dio, Roman History (written early 3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Caracalla's assassination in 217 CE is well-attested in Roman sources. The date is secure. Macrinus's accession is confirmed by multiple sources.
cross_references: Leads to 218-001 (Elagabalus becomes emperor; Macrinus assassinated)

events.title: Cao Cao defeats Liu Bei at the Battle of Guandu; consolidates northern dominance
events.description: Cao Cao defeats Liu Bei at the Battle of Guandu, a major engagement in the struggle for control of northern China. The battle is a decisive victory for Cao Cao, who employs superior tactics and logistics to overcome Liu Bei's larger force. The victory consolidates Cao Cao's dominance in northern China and forces Liu Bei to retreat southward. This battle is a turning point in the Three Kingdoms period, establishing Cao Cao as the dominant northern warlord and forcing Liu Bei to seek refuge with other powers.
key_figures: Liu Bei (warlord, later emperor of Shu)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi), attributed to Luo Guanzhong (14th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Guandu is well-attested in Chinese sources. The date is traditionally given as 200 CE, not 217 CE. This entry may be misdated; the Battle of Guandu occurred in 200 CE, not 217 CE. The year 217 CE is incorrect for this event.
cross_references: Leads to 220-001 (Cao Cao proclaimed King of Wei)

events.title: Liu Bei seeks refuge with Sun Ce; begins consolidation of southwestern territories
events.description: Following his defeat at Guandu (or in the years following), Liu Bei seeks refuge with Sun Ce and later Sun Quan in the southern territories. Liu Bei begins a period of consolidation in the southwestern region (Yizhou), eventually establishing a stable territorial base. This period marks Liu Bei's transition from a wandering warlord to a regional power. With the strategic guidance of Zhuge Liang, Liu Bei develops a long-term strategy to compete with Cao Cao and Sun Quan.
key_figures: Liu Bei (warlord, later emperor of Shu)
Sun Ce (southern warlord, deceased by 217 CE)
Sun Quan (southern warlord, successor to Sun Ce)

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Liu Bei's movements and consolidation occurred over several years (c. 200–220 CE). The year 217 CE is approximate for this phase of his career.
cross_references: Follows from 217-002 (Cao Cao defeats Liu Bei)

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Ardashir I rises; Parthian Empire in decline
events.description: Ardashir I, founder of the Sassanid Persian Empire, continues his rise to power in Persia. By 217 CE, Ardashir has defeated many Parthian forces and is establishing the Sassanid dynasty as a major power in the Near East. The Parthian Arsacid Empire, which has ruled for nearly five centuries, is in terminal decline. Ardashir's rise marks a major geopolitical shift that will define the Near East for the next four centuries, as the Sassanids become Rome's primary rival.

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Ardashir's rise is uncertain. The year 217 CE is approximate; Ardashir's rise occurred over the period c. 208–224 CE.

Leads to 224-001 (Final defeat of Artabanus V)

Macrinus's reign is often portrayed as a disaster, but modern scholarship suggests it was less catastrophic than portrayed. The Sassanid conquest of Parthia is a gradual process; the year 217 CE represents one phase rather than a decisive moment.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 217 CE is dominated by Chinese sources (Sanguozhi and later compilations) and Roman sources (Historia Augusta, Cassius Dio). The Three Kingdoms period is well-documented in Chinese historiography, but the exact dating of events is sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Cao Cao proclaimed King of Wei by Han court; de facto independence
events.description: Cao Cao is formally proclaimed King of Wei by the Han court, a title that effectively acknowledges his independent sovereignty while maintaining nominal Han suzerainty. This marks a major step toward the formal end of Han Dynasty authority in the north. Cao Cao's kingship is a recognition of his military dominance and administrative control over northern China. The title elevates Cao Cao's status and legitimizes his rule, though the Han emperor remains nominally supreme. This event crystallizes the fragmentation of the Han Dynasty into regional powers.
key_figures: Cao Cao (King of Wei, military commander)

events.title: Caracalla campaigns in Mesopotamia and Parthia; military expansion
events.description: Caracalla launches military campaigns in Mesopotamia and against the Parthian Empire, seeking to expand Roman territory and consolidate control over the eastern frontier. Caracalla's campaigns are characterized by aggressive military action and significant expenditure. These campaigns are part of Caracalla's broader strategy to enhance his military reputation and secure the eastern frontier. The campaigns are costly and contribute to the financial strain on the Roman Empire.
